title: F1-Score: The Balancing Act
concept: The F1-score combines precision and recall into a single metric, helping evaluate models when both false alarms and missed cases are important.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In a confusion matrix, what happens when a model fails to detect an actual positive event?

options.text: It registers as a False Negative
It registers as a False Positive
It is counted as a True Negative

exercises.explain: Correct! A missed positive event is a False Negative.

cards.title: The tug-of-war
cards.body: If you adjust your spam filter to block more spam (high recall), you start blocking real emails (low precision). It's a constant tug-of-war. How do we measure the balance?

cards.title: What is the F1-score?
cards.body: The F1-score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall. It combines both into a single number between 0 and 1, where 1 is perfect balance and 0 is complete failure.

cards.title: Average height vs volume
cards.body: Think of it like balancing a table's legs. If one leg is extremely short (e.g., 0% precision) and the other is long (100% recall), a normal average hides the wobble. The F1-score exposes the imbalance.

cards.title: Calculating F1
cards.body: If a model has 100% recall but only 10% precision, its regular average is 55%. But its F1-score is only 18%! It penalizes extreme imbalances, giving you a truer picture of performance.

cards.title: Myth: F1-score is always better than accuracy
cards.body: F1 is great for imbalanced data. But if your classes are perfectly balanced (50/50) and false alarms cost the same as missed cases, raw accuracy is still easier to explain and use.

cards.title: Comparing metrics
cards.body: How F1-score handles imbalanced models compared to a simple average:

exercises.prompt: Why is the F1-score useful for evaluating models?

options.text: It balances precision and recall, preventing a model with terrible precision from looking good
It only counts correct predictions and ignores errors
It makes models train twice as fast
It works for both classification and clustering

exercises.explain: The F1-score is low if either precision or recall is low, forcing the model to perform well on both.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A model with 0% precision and 100% recall will have a 50% F1-score.
exercises.explain: False. Because F1 uses a harmonic mean, if either metric is 0%, the F1-score drops to 0%.

exercises.sentence: The F1-score is the harmonic mean of precision and ___.
exercises.explain: The F1-score combines precision and recall to balance false alarms and missed cases.

exercises.prompt: For which of these models would the F1-score be the most helpful evaluation metric?

options.text: A rare disease diagnostic model where sick patients are 0.5% of the data
A model predicting coin flips where heads and tails are exactly 50/50
A regression model predicting the exact price of gold over time

exercises.explain: For rare events (imbalanced data), the F1-score is critical to ensure we don't cheat using raw accuracy.

exercises.prompt: You compare two fraud detection models. Model A has 90% precision and 10% recall. Model B has 50% precision and 50% recall. Which model has a higher F1-score?

options.text: Model B, because its balanced performance results in a higher F1-score (~50%) than Model A's (~18%).
Model A, because 90% precision is much higher than 50%.
Both have the same F1-score because their averages are identical.
Model A, because fraud detection only cares about precision.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Model A has a terrible recall of 10%, which severely drags down its F1-score. Model B is more balanced and practical. 🎉
